skillName: Use and interpret inequalities in context (incl number lines and statements)
misconceptionSummary: Students compare negatives by face value, not position — thinking -8 > -3 because 8 > 3.

steps.narration: Look at where the two numbers sit on the line. The arrow points right — from the smaller number towards the larger one. On a number line, right always means bigger.

visuals.ruleText: Further left = smaller. Further right = larger.

steps.narration: This rule holds for every number — positive, negative, zero, decimal. The number line never lies. Position on the line is the only thing that matters.
Use the line position to write the symbol. If you can picture where both numbers sit, the symbol almost writes itself.

visuals.label: Closer to zero = greater, for negative numbers

steps.narration: Negative four is closer to zero than negative nine, so it sits further right — and further right always means greater.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking -8 > -3 because 8 > 3. For negatives, the larger the absolute value, the smaller the number.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: With negatives, bigger digits mean further from zero — which means further left — which means smaller. Flip your instinct.
